prompt:
Ukiyo-e woodblock print. Katsushika Hokusai, Fugaku Sanjūrokkei series, circa 1831. Museum archival reproduction quality.

═══ PHYSICAL PRINT SIMULATION ═══
Simulate the exact physical artifact of a multi-block Edo woodblock print:
- Key block (outline block): hand-carved black ink lines with visible 
  chisel drag — lines taper at endpoints, thicken at pressure points, 
  show micro-splits at sharp turns. Not digitally smooth. Not calligraphy-pen 
  smooth. Carved hardwood tool marks.
- Color blocks: each flat color area was printed from a SEPARATE block. 
  Simulate slight misregistration (0.5–1mm offset) between color fill 
  and outline on 1–2 elements only — a hallmark of authentic hand-printing.
- Woodgrain texture: appears ONLY inside the ochre sky band and grey-blue 
  sea bands — these are where the baren pressed soft wood grain into 
  dampened washi paper. The tree silhouette, foliage, and figures 
  have NO woodgrain overlay whatsoever.
- Paper: ivory-cream washi ground with subtle fiber texture visible 
  in unprinted margins and white areas only.

═══ COMPOSITION ═══
Asymmetric diagonal. A weathered coastal black pine (kuromatsu) dominates 
center-left, rooted in a dark basalt promontory outcropping. The tree 
leans dramatically right under sea wind. Mount Fuji rises faint and small 
in the upper-left distance — a ghost silhouette in pale grey-blue, 
snow cap in reserved ivory. Lower third: three horizontal bands of 
grey-blue sea, each a slightly different value, separated by thin white 
bokashi gradation lines. Upper two-thirds: flat ochre sky with woodgrain.

═══ DECISION TREE STRUCTURE ═══
The pine IS the decision tree. Every structural element of the tree 
corresponds to a data structure component — no overlay, no diagram, 
no separate visual language.

ROOT NODE: The trunk base. No text on the trunk surface. Instead, 
a single hand-carved wooden ema plaque (votive tablet shape — 
pentagonal, beveled top) is nailed to the trunk with visible iron 
tacks. On it: ONE vertical column of 5 Japanese characters in 
authentic Edo brushwork — the strokes must appear brushed onto 
wood grain, then carved in relief, showing ink absorption into 
the wood surface. The characters should visually read as 
calligraphically coherent even if simplified.

DECISION NODES (branch forks): At each major bifurcation, the branch 
naturally thickens into a gnarled knot — this IS the decision node. 
From each knot, a small rectangular fuda (label tag) hangs on a 
short cord, carved in the same relief style as the ema. One fuda 
per fork, one short character column per fuda.

BRANCH VISUAL LOGIC (critical for concept legibility):
- LEFT branch at every fork = the "yes" path = branches grow upward 
  at a steeper angle, foliage clusters are DARKER pine green
- RIGHT branch at every fork = the "no" path = branches extend more 
  horizontally, foliage clusters are LIGHTER muted sage green
- This left/dark/up vs right/light/horizontal rule applies 
  consistently at every level of the tree

BRANCH PHYSICS:
- Trunk: thickest, deeply furrowed bark texture in key-block lines
- Generation 1 branches: 60% trunk width
- Generation 2 branches: 40% trunk width
- Generation 3 twigs: thin single-chisel-width lines
- All curves are multi-faceted angular approximations of curves 
  (as actual wood carving produces) — no smooth bezier arcs

LEAF NODES: Terminal twig clusters end in stylized pine needle 
bursts (sensu fan-shaped radiating lines). Each terminal cluster 
frames a mon (family crest) in its center — the needles GROW 
AROUND the mon as if the crest grew with the tree. 
One terminal cluster on the lowest right branch has needles 
rendered in vermillion red — the anomalous/misclassification node.

═══ COLOR PALETTE (strict) ═══
FORBIDDEN: Any color not on this list.
- Sumi black: outlines, carved text, branch silhouette
- Prussian blue (Bero-ai): deep trunk and branch fill
- Muted pine green (two values: dark and sage): foliage only
- Flat ochre yellow: sky band only
- Three values of grey-blue: sea bands only
- Vermillion red: one anomalous leaf cluster + seal cartouche only
- Ivory white: Fuji silhouette, horizon mist, paper margins, 
  unprinted washi areas

═══ SEALS AND CARTOUCHES ═══
Bottom-right corner: TWO seals stacked vertically, authentic 1830s format:
1. Publisher seal (hanmoto-in): rectangular, red, two-character content
2. Censor seal (kiwame-in): small oval, red, single character — 
   this seal was legally required on approved prints 1790–1842

NO other text. NO titles. NO English. NO floating labels. 
NO speech bubbles. NO modern typography of any kind.

═══ ABSOLUTE PROHIBITIONS ═══
- No photographic shading or gradients on any object
- No glow, bloom, drop shadow, or layer effects
- No 3D rendering or perspective depth cues
- No uniform canvas texture overlay
- No smooth digital vector curves on branches
- No AI-garbled characters — if calligraphy cannot be rendered 
  authentically, use abstract brushstroke marks that READ as 
  calligraphic rather than legible-but-wrong characters
- No Western art conventions of any kind
- No misregistration on more than 2 elements
tradition_approach: Hokusai compositional diagonals, actual pine tree as decision tree metaphor, branch forks as splits, flat indigo-ochre-vermillion palette, woodgrain texture, bold outlines, tategaki script labels, zero shading.
